title: Gerunds and Infinitives in Everyday Life

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
One of the trickiest parts of English grammar is knowing when to use the <gerund> (-ing form) and when to use the <infinitive> (to + verb) after another verb. Native speakers use these patterns naturally, but for learners, it requires practice.

In this reading, we'll explore gerunds and infinitives through real-life situations — conversations, emails, and everyday decisions — so you can see how these patterns work in context.

sections.title: Situation 1: Planning a Weekend
sections.content:
Read this conversation between two friends:

Sarah: I <suggest> going to the new Italian restaurant on Saturday.
Mark: Sounds good! I <enjoy> eating Italian food. But I <want> to go early — I <hate> waiting for a table.
Sarah: I <agree> to meet at 7pm. After dinner, do you <fancy> watching a film?
Mark: I'd <prefer> to go for a walk instead. I <need> to get more exercise.
Sarah: OK, I don't <mind> walking. I'll <avoid> wearing heels then!

Notice the patterns:
• <suggest>, <enjoy>, <fancy>, <mind>, <avoid> → followed by <gerund> (-ing)
• <want>, <agree>, <prefer>, <need> → followed by <infinitive> (to + verb)
• <hate> → can take both forms (hate waiting / hate to wait)

sections.title: Situation 2: A Work Email
sections.content:
Read this professional email:

Dear Team,

I <hope> to share some updates about our project. We <managed> to complete the first phase on time. I <appreciate> everyone working so hard.

However, we <need> to discuss the timeline. I <suggest> scheduling a meeting this week. Please <avoid> booking anything for Thursday afternoon.

I <look forward to> hearing your thoughts. Don't <hesitate> to contact me if you have questions.

Best regards,
Lisa

Key patterns in professional English:
• <hope>, <manage>, <need>, <hesitate> → <infinitive> (to + verb)
• <appreciate>, <suggest>, <avoid> → <gerund> (-ing)
• <look forward to> → <gerund> (because <to> is a preposition here, not part of an infinitive)

sections.title: Situation 3: Talking About Habits
sections.content:
Read how people describe their daily habits:

Tom: I <tend> to wake up early. I <enjoy> running before work. I <can't stand> sitting at a desk all day, so I <try> to take breaks.

Ana: I <love> cooking dinner for my family. I <keep> trying new recipes. I <refuse> to eat fast food during the week.

James: I <admit> spending too much time on my phone. I <need> to stop checking social media. I <plan> to read more books this year.

Useful patterns for talking about habits:
• <tend>, <try>, <refuse>, <plan>, <need> → <infinitive>
• <enjoy>, <can't stand>, <keep>, <admit> → <gerund>
• <love> → both forms work (love cooking / love to cook)
• <stop> → changes meaning! <stop checking> = quit the habit; <stop to check> = pause in order to check

sections.title: Verbs That Change Meaning
sections.content:
Some verbs change their meaning depending on whether they are followed by a gerund or an infinitive. This is an important distinction:

<Remember>:
• I <remember> locking the door. (= I have a memory of doing it)
• I <remembered> to lock the door. (= I didn't forget to do it)

<Stop>:
• She <stopped> smoking. (= She quit the habit)
• She <stopped> to smoke. (= She paused what she was doing in order to smoke)

<Try>:
• <Try> opening the window. (= experiment with this method)
• <Try> to open the window. (= make an effort/attempt)

<Forget>:
• I'll never <forget> meeting you. (= I have a memory of the event)
• I <forgot> to meet you. (= I didn't remember the appointment)

These verbs require extra attention because the wrong form can communicate the opposite of what you mean!

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Key takeaways from this reading:

• <Gerund> verbs (enjoy, avoid, suggest, mind, keep, finish, consider, deny, admit, imagine) often describe reactions, feelings, or ongoing situations.
• <Infinitive> verbs (want, decide, hope, plan, agree, refuse, offer, promise, manage, expect) often express intentions, desires, or future-oriented actions.
• Some verbs (like, love, hate, start, begin, continue, prefer) accept <both> forms with similar meaning.
• After <prepositions> (interested in, good at, look forward to), always use the gerund.
• A few verbs (remember, stop, try, forget) <change meaning> depending on the form used.
• The best way to learn these patterns is through exposure and practice in real contexts.

learningObjectives: Recognize gerund and infinitive patterns in real-life contexts
Understand how these patterns appear in conversations and emails
Identify verbs that change meaning with gerund vs. infinitive
Apply gerund and infinitive rules to describe habits and plans
Distinguish between preposition + gerund and infinitive structures

keyVocabulary.definition: The -ing form of a verb used as a noun (e.g., swimming, reading)
The base form of a verb with 'to' (e.g., to swim, to read)
The grammatical structure that follows a verb
When a verb has different meanings with gerund vs. infinitive
keyVocabulary.example: Stop smoking (quit) vs. stop to smoke (pause)
